_header.purpose: Per-endpoint RED method dashboard with Rate, Errors, and Duration breakdowns using template variables
_header.scope: Per-endpoint HTTP metrics analysis with template variables for method and path filtering
_header.overview: Implements the RED (Rate, Errors, Duration) method for backend API endpoint analysis. Provides per-endpoint breakdowns using Grafana template variables for HTTP method and path filtering. Includes request rate by path, error rate by path, duration histogram heatmap, and exemplar links to Tempo traces. Enables drill-down from aggregate Web Summary to specific endpoint performance.
_header.dependencies: Mimir datasource (uid: mimir), Tempo datasource (uid: tempo) for exemplar links, backend metrics instruments
_header.interfaces: PromQL queries with template variables $method and $path for endpoint filtering
_header.related: web-summary.json for aggregate view, web-traces.json for trace drill-down, backend metrics.py for metric definitions
_header.implementation: Grafana dashboard JSON schema version 39 with template variables and exemplar-enabled queries

title: RED
description: The RED method applied per-endpoint. Use the Method and Path dropdowns above to filter. Rate shows which endpoints are busiest. Errors shows which endpoints are failing. Duration shows which endpoints are slowest. During an incident, filter to the suspected endpoint and check all three signals. The exemplar panel at the bottom lets you click a data point to jump directly to the trace that caused it — this is the fastest path from 'something is slow' to 'here is exactly why'.

options.content:
# RED (Rate, Errors, Duration)
The RED method applied per-endpoint. Use the Method and Path dropdowns to filter. Rate shows which endpoints are busiest. Errors shows which are failing. Duration shows which are slowest. The exemplar panel at the bottom lets you click a data point to jump directly to the trace that caused it.

panels.title: Request Rate by Path
panels.description: Requests per second broken down by URL path. Identifies which endpoints carry the most traffic. A path that suddenly appears or spikes may indicate a new caller or retry loop. Compare with the error and duration panels to find the problematic path.

panels.title: Request Rate by Method
panels.description: Traffic volume by HTTP method (GET, POST, etc.). Stacked bars show the method mix over time. An unusual method ratio (e.g., spike in POSTs during normally GET-heavy traffic) may indicate automated traffic or an attack.

panels.title: Error Rate % by Path
panels.description: Error percentage per endpoint. Quickly identifies which specific endpoint is failing. A single path at 100% while others are green means a localized bug or missing resource. All paths rising together means a systemic issue (database down, shared dependency failing).

panels.title: Error Count by Status Code
panels.description: Errors per second grouped by HTTP status code for the selected endpoints. Tells you the failure mode: 500 = server crash, 502/503 = infrastructure issue, 404 = missing resource, 422 = validation failure, 429 = rate limiting. The dominant status code guides your investigation.

panels.title: Duration p50/p95/p99 by Path
panels.description: Latency percentiles per endpoint. Find the slow endpoint: which path has the highest p99? Use the Path filter to isolate it. If one path is slow while others are fast, the problem is in that endpoint's code or downstream call. If all paths are slow, the system is uniformly degraded.

panels.title: Request Duration Heatmap
panels.description: Distribution of request durations over time. Each cell's color intensity shows how many requests fell in that latency bucket. A healthy system shows a dense band at the bottom (fast requests). A band appearing higher up means a new population of slow requests. Bimodal bands (two distinct layers) suggest two different code paths with different performance characteristics.

panels.title: Request Duration with Exemplars
panels.description: P95 and P99 latency with clickable exemplar dots. Each dot is a real request — click it to open the full distributed trace in Tempo. This is the fastest way to go from 'latency is high' to 'here is the exact trace showing why'. Look for dots that are far above the line — those are the outlier requests to investigate first.

panels.title: Top Endpoints by Request Count
panels.description: The 10 most-called endpoints in the selected time range, sorted by total request count. Use this to understand traffic distribution — which endpoints get the most load. An endpoint that suddenly appears in the top 10 may indicate a new traffic source or retry loop.
